items.title: Viscous soluble fiber add-on, such as psyllium, if tolerated
items.timing: Risk model · Metabolic risk / diabetes prevention · small effect · biomarker rct
items.do: Viscous soluble fiber add-on, such as psyllium, if tolerated. Consider a food-or-supplement soluble fiber intervention focused on LDL/non-HDL and postprandial glycemia.
items.why: Consider a food-or-supplement soluble fiber intervention focused on LDL/non-HDL and postprandial glycemia.
items.goal: Domain opportunity: high (~35% baseline risk × high residual opportunity). Downside: low.

orders.label: Viscous soluble fiber add-on, such as psyllium, if tolerated

items.evidence: Human biomarker evidence supports modest LDL-C lowering and improved postprandial glycemic response with viscous soluble fibers. PMIDs: 41366295, 37163454.
items.author: meridian-v0.5

items.title: Portfolio-style food substitution focused on plant sterols, viscous fiber, nuts, and soy protein
items.timing: Risk model · Cardiovascular risk optimization · small effect · biomarker rct
items.do: Portfolio-style food substitution focused on plant sterols, viscous fiber, nuts, and soy protein. Use a defined food-substitution pattern as an optional LDL/ApoB-lowering nutrition adjunct.
items.why: Use a defined food-substitution pattern as an optional LDL/ApoB-lowering nutrition adjunct.
items.goal: Domain opportunity: moderate-high (~7.5% baseline risk × moderate-high residual opportunity). Downside: low.

orders.label: Portfolio-style food substitution focused on plant sterols, viscous fiber, nuts, and soy protein

items.evidence: Human dietary-intervention evidence shows that combinations of cholesterol-lowering foods can improve LDL-related biomarkers. PMIDs: 40152922, 40879583.
items.author: meridian-v0.5

items.title: Nitrate-rich vegetable or beetroot trial for BP and exercise-response biomarkers
items.timing: Risk model · Cardiovascular risk optimization · small effect · biomarker rct
items.do: Nitrate-rich vegetable or beetroot trial for BP and exercise-response biomarkers. A short, monitored trial of nitrate-rich foods could be considered for borderline-above-optimal BP and low CRF.
items.why: A short, monitored trial of nitrate-rich foods could be considered for borderline-above-optimal BP and low CRF.
items.goal: Domain opportunity: moderate-high (~7.5% baseline risk × moderate-high residual opportunity). Downside: low.

orders.label: Nitrate-rich vegetable or beetroot trial for BP and exercise-response biomarkers

items.evidence: Human biomarker studies show that dietary nitrate can modestly influence nitric-oxide pathways, BP, and exercise efficiency in some populations. PMIDs: 41469734.
items.author: meridian-v0.5

items.title: Higher-intensity interval exposure trial layered onto current cycling if safe and acceptable
items.timing: Risk model · Metabolic risk / diabetes prevention · moderate effect · close analog
items.do: Higher-intensity interval exposure trial layered onto current cycling if safe and acceptable. Evaluate a cautious interval-training option using her existing indoor cycling habit to improve VO2max.
items.why: Evaluate a cautious interval-training option using her existing indoor cycling habit to improve VO2max.
items.goal: Domain opportunity: high (~35% baseline risk × high residual opportunity). Downside: low.

orders.label: Higher-intensity interval exposure trial layered onto current cycling if safe and acceptable

items.evidence: Human exercise studies show interval training can improve VO2max efficiently in many adults. PMIDs: 41622107, 34684080.
items.author: meridian-v0.5

items.title: Alcohol-free sleep and glucose n-of-1 trial
items.timing: Risk model · Cardiovascular risk optimization · small effect · low downside only
items.do: Alcohol-free sleep and glucose n-of-1 trial. Run a defined alcohol-free or near-alcohol-free trial period with wearable sleep and, if available, glucose markers to see whether sleep efficiency, resting HR, HRV, BP, or next-day glucose patterns improve.
items.why: Run a defined alcohol-free or near-alcohol-free trial period with wearable sleep and, if available, glucose markers to see whether sleep efficiency, resting HR, HRV, BP, or next-day glucose patterns improve.
items.goal: Domain opportunity: moderate-high (~7.5% baseline risk × moderate-high residual opportunity). Downside: low.

orders.label: Alcohol-free sleep and glucose n-of-1 trial

items.evidence: Human epidemiology and biomarker evidence links alcohol intake with sleep fragmentation, BP, triglycerides, glycemia, and breast cancer risk in dose-dependent ways. PMIDs: 34984504, 33856043.
items.author: meridian-v0.5

items.title: Digital CBT-I or structured sleep-efficiency protocol
items.timing: Risk model · Neurocognitive / sleep and stress risk · moderate effect · biomarker rct
items.do: Digital CBT-I or structured sleep-efficiency protocol. Consider a structured behavioral sleep program to improve sleep efficiency and duration.
items.why: Consider a structured behavioral sleep program to improve sleep efficiency and duration.
items.goal: Domain opportunity: low-moderate. Downside: low.

orders.label: Digital CBT-I or structured sleep-efficiency protocol

items.evidence: Human evidence supports behavioral sleep interventions for improving sleep efficiency and insomnia symptoms, with downstream biomarker effects on mood, glucose regulation, and BP plausible but less definitive in this exact patient state. PMIDs: 39316400, 39023982.
items.author: meridian-v0.5

items.title: Morning bright-light and evening light-hygiene routine for circadian reinforcement
items.timing: Risk model · Neurocognitive / sleep and stress risk · small effect · mechanism only
items.do: Morning bright-light and evening light-hygiene routine for circadian reinforcement. A low-downside circadian intervention could be tested to support earlier sleep consolidation and improve sleep efficiency.
items.why: A low-downside circadian intervention could be tested to support earlier sleep consolidation and improve sleep efficiency.
items.goal: Domain opportunity: low-moderate. Downside: low.

orders.label: Morning bright-light and evening light-hygiene routine for circadian reinforcement

items.evidence: Human sleep and circadian biomarker evidence supports light timing as a regulator of circadian phase and sleep quality. PMIDs: 37754349, 23372241.
items.author: meridian-v0.5

items.title: HRV biofeedback or paced-breathing stress-recovery practice
items.timing: Risk model · Neurocognitive / sleep and stress risk · small effect · biomarker rct
items.do: HRV biofeedback or paced-breathing stress-recovery practice. Use a monitorable autonomic-regulation practice given high perceived stress, mild anxiety symptoms, and low-normal wearable HRV.
items.why: Use a monitorable autonomic-regulation practice given high perceived stress, mild anxiety symptoms, and low-normal wearable HRV.
items.goal: Domain opportunity: low-moderate. Downside: low.

orders.label: HRV biofeedback or paced-breathing stress-recovery practice

items.evidence: Human biomarker studies suggest paced breathing and HRV biofeedback can influence autonomic measures, perceived stress, and sometimes BP. PMIDs: 36917418, 36831799.
items.author: meridian-v0.5

items.title: Food-first omega-3 assessment pathway with optional omega-3 index testing
items.timing: Risk model · Cardiovascular risk optimization · small effect · mechanism only
items.do: Food-first omega-3 assessment pathway with optional omega-3 index testing. Consider measuring omega-3 index only if it would change behavior, such as increasing fatty fish intake or considering supplementation.
items.why: Consider measuring omega-3 index only if it would change behavior, such as increasing fatty fish intake or considering supplementation.
items.goal: Domain opportunity: moderate-high (~7.5% baseline risk × moderate-high residual opportunity). Downside: low.

orders.label: Food-first omega-3 assessment pathway with optional omega-3 index testing

items.evidence: Human biomarker evidence links omega-3 status to triglyceride and inflammatory pathways, but routine omega-3 index testing and supplement decisions for primary prevention are not universally standard-of-care. PMIDs: 34680583, 34911587.
items.author: meridian-v0.5

items.title: Sauna or passive heat exposure protocol with BP precautions
items.timing: Risk model · Cardiovascular risk optimization · uncertain effect · human observational plus mechanism
items.do: Sauna or passive heat exposure protocol with BP precautions. A carefully monitored passive heat exposure option could be considered if she is interested and has no contraindicating symptoms.
items.why: A carefully monitored passive heat exposure option could be considered if she is interested and has no contraindicating symptoms.
items.goal: Domain opportunity: moderate-high (~7.5% baseline risk × moderate-high residual opportunity). Downside: medium.

orders.label: Sauna or passive heat exposure protocol with BP precautions

items.evidence: Human epidemiologic and physiologic evidence associates sauna use with vascular, BP, and stress-response markers, but causality and patient-specific outcome benefit are uncertain. PMIDs: 38577299, 37248758.
items.author: meridian-v0.5

items.title: H. pylori noninvasive testing as optional gastric-risk clarification
items.timing: Risk model · Cancer / hereditary genetics prevention and screening · uncertain effect · mechanism only
items.do: H. pylori noninvasive testing as optional gastric-risk clarification. Consider noninvasive H.
items.why: Consider noninvasive H.
items.goal: Domain opportunity: moderate. Downside: medium.

orders.label: H. pylori noninvasive testing as optional gastric-risk clarification

items.evidence: Human evidence links H. PMIDs: 34976604, 39286646.
items.author: meridian-v0.5
items.domain: Cancer / hereditary genetics prevention and screening

excluded.name: Routine ovarian cancer screening with CA-125 and transvaginal ultrasound
Risk-reducing bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy solely for ovarian cancer family history
excluded.reason: Exclude unless new genetic or specialist risk information changes eligibility.

excluded.name: Whole-body MRI as broad cancer or longevity screening
Multi-cancer early detection blood test as a substitute for indicated screening
Amyloid PET, brain MRI, or plasma neurodegeneration biomarkers for asymptomatic dementia screening
excluded.reason: Exclude as research-only or non-actionable in this payload.

excluded.name: Carotid intima-media thickness screening as a CVD reclassifier
excluded.reason: Exclude or deprioritize compared with better-supported CVD risk clarification.

excluded.name: GLP-1 receptor agonist solely for prediabetes in normal-BMI patient
excluded.reason: Exclude as a non-indicated medication based on supplied data.

excluded.name: SGLT2 inhibitor for kidney or cardiovascular prevention without diabetes, CKD, or heart failure
excluded.reason: Exclude as non-indicated from the supplied facts.

SoC Monitoring.title: Initiate annual breast MRI surveillance in addition to mammography
Order baseline DEXA bone density screening
Monitor renal function, albuminuria, and ACE-inhibitor safety labs

SoC Risk Reduction.title: Enroll in or emulate a structured Diabetes Prevention Program
Prevent further postmenopausal weight gain and reduce central adiposity
Shift carbohydrate quality and meal composition for prediabetes

Adjunct Options.title: Structured post-meal walking after higher-carbohydrate meals
Short-term continuous glucose monitor experiment for post-meal pattern discovery
Meal-sequencing experiment: vegetables and protein before starch
